description_long: This object is a cylindrical stick grenade, commonly known as a 'potato masher' due to its distinctive shape. The main body consists of a long, slightly tapered wooden handle attached to a cylindrical metal head, which is likely the explosive charge housing. The head is dark, possibly painted or coated metal, and features white stenciled text, which may include manufacturing or warning information. The handle appears to be made of wood, showing a natural brown color and subtle grain, with metal bands or caps at both ends for reinforcement. The overall construction suggests a robust, utilitarian design intended for manual throwing. The object is compact and streamlined, with no visible external wiring or complex mechanisms.
description: A stick grenade with a cylindrical metal head and a long wooden handle, featuring stenciled text on the head. The design is robust and utilitarian, with metal and wood components.
description_view_0: Front view showing the full length of the grenade, with the cylindrical head and stenciled text clearly visible.
description_view_1: Angled view highlighting the connection between the wooden handle and the metal head, with text partially visible.
description_view_2: Top-down view focusing on the circular end of the metal head, with the handle extending downward and minimal detail visible.
description_view_3: Rear angled view showing the length of the handle and the back of the metal head, with no visible text.
attribute_detonation_mechanism: The detonation mechanism is likely initiated by unscrewing the end cap and pulling a cord or striker, as suggested by the traditional design of stick grenades; no external buttons or switches are visible.
